setup_task.sh:42 'from registry.models import Aircraft'; verifier.py line 39 confirms this import

setup_task.sh:52-56 checks 'ac.operator'; Aerobridge GitHub models.py confirms ForeignKey to Operator named 'operator'

setup_task.sh:53 directly checks 'ac.type_certificate'; but Aerobridge GitHub Aircraft model (openskies-sh/aerobridge/registry/models.py) has NO direct type_certificate field. The Aircraft model has only: manufacturer (ForeignKey), operator (ForeignKey), name (CharField), flight_controller_id, status, final_assembly (OneToOneField to AircraftAssembly), photo (URLField), and metadata fields. Type certificate is accessible only through nested relationship: Aircraft.final_assembly.aircraft_model.type_certificate

privileged_info_summary: The task requires agents to write a Django script that audits Aircraft compliance. The script paths, required Django imports, and access to manufacturer and operator fields are all correctly specified. However, the compliance criteria includes 'type_certificate' as a direct field on Aircraft objects, but the actual Aerobridge Aircraft model from GitHub does not have a direct type_certificate attribute. Aircraft can only access type_certificate through a nested chain: Aircraft → final_assembly → AircraftAssembly → aircraft_model → AircraftModel → type_certificate. The setup script's direct check 'ac.type_certificate' would raise an AttributeError when executed against the actual Aerobridge database.
